### Underground Storage Tanks
#### Owners and Operators
[ Notification of Change (Facility, Owner, Product, Service) ](https://deq.mt.gov/twr/assistance#accordion3-collapse1)
##### Notification is required when:
  * Starting a new underground storage tank facility
  * Installing or modifying tanks or piping
  * Changing or amending facility, tank and/or ownership information
  * Placing a tank into inactive or active status
  * Changing the tank product
  * Tanks are found[](https://deq.mt.gov/files/Land/UST/Documents/PDFfiles/DiscoveredTanks.pdf)


##### New System/Facility
  * [Notification of Underground Storage Tanks (New System)](https://deq.mt.gov/files/Land/UST/Documents/PDFfiles/NotificationofUndergroundStorageTanksFull.pdf)


##### Facility Changes - complete within 30 days
  * [Notification of Underground Storage Tanks (Facility Change)](https://deq.mt.gov/files/Land/UST/Documents/PDFfiles/NotificationforUndergroundStorageTanksOwnerChangewithFID.pdf)[](https://deq.mt.gov/files/Land/UST/Documents/PDFfiles/NotificationforUndergroundStorageTanksOwnerChangewithFID.pdf)
  * [Notification of Inactive Status](https://deq.mt.gov/files/Land/UST/Documents/PDFfiles/NotifyofInactiveStatus.pdf)


##### New or Change of Owner
We can help you as the new owner satisfy all requirements. In short, must fill out required forms, pay registration fees, meet compliance and complete regular inspections, have a permitted operator, and demonstrate financial responsibility. 
  * [New or Change of Owner Checklist](https://deq.mt.gov/files/Land/UST/Documents/PDFfiles/NewOwnerInfoChecklist.pdf)
  * [Amended of Change of Owner Form](https://deq.mt.gov/files/Land/UST/Documents/PDFfiles/NotificationforUndergroundStorageTanksOwnerChangewithFID.pdf) ([](https://deq.mt.gov/files/Land/UST/Documents/PDFfiles/NotificationforUndergroundStorageTanksOwnerChangewithFID.pdf)complete within 30 days)


* **Buyer disclaimer:** When a person buys property, they are buying responsibility for any existing contamination as well. Check with [Petroleum Tank Cleanup Section](https://deq.mt.gov/cleanupandrec/Programs/petrocleanup) on releases.
##### Tank Product Change - complete within 30 days
  * [Product Type Change Notification Form](https://deq.mt.gov/files/Land/UST/Documents/PDFfiles/NewApplications/ProductTypeChange.pdf)


##### Change of Service
  * To complete this procedure owners or operators must notify the department at least 30 days before making a change in service,**and;**
  * Empty and clean the UST system by removing all liquid, accumulated sludge and all combustible and flammable vapors,**and;**
  * Conduct a site assessment in accordance with[ARM 17.56.703.](http://www.mtrules.org/gateway/RuleNo.asp?RN=17%2E56%2E703)


##### Discovered Tanks - notify within 30 days (fill out both forms)
  * [PTRCB Discovered Tanks](https://deq.mt.gov/files/Land/UST/Documents/PDFfiles/DiscoveredTanks.pdf)
  * [NotificationofUndergroundStorageTanks](https://deq.mt.gov/files/Land/UST/Documents/PDFfiles/NotificationofUndergroundStorageTanksFull.pdf)


[ Tank Registration Fees ](https://deq.mt.gov/twr/assistance#accordion3-collapse2)
In order to get your operator permit, tank registration fees must be paid. Please visit the [Montana Department of Labor and Industry eStop program](https://aca-prod.accela.com/ESTOP/Default.aspx) to pay registration fees.
To contact eStop Business Licenses:
  * Email:DLIEstop@mt.gov
  * Phone: 406-841-2056


[ Compliance for Owners and Operators ](https://deq.mt.gov/twr/assistance#accordion3-collapse3)
Issuance of an operating permit is based on a compliance inspection that indicates that the UST is in compliance with Montana's operation and maintenance requirements.
Licensed private inspectors conduct the compliance inspections of operating underground storage tanks to determine compliance with spill, overfill, corrosion protection, release detection and recordkeeping requirements. They also verify that all tanks are registered, have the proper tags and operating permit, and meet upgrade requirements.
The department will provide oversight of the licensed compliance inspectors.
Monthly and annual walkthrough inspections are required. Visit the [Resources page for compliance guidance and forms](https://deq.mt.gov/twr/resources#accordion4-collapse2%20).
[ Operator Training and TankHelper ](https://deq.mt.gov/twr/assistance#accordion3-collapse4)
Please notify DEQ's UST program of any class A or B operator changes to your facility. 
##### Minimum Training Requirements and Training Options 
  * [Class A, B and C Training in Montana](https://deq.mt.gov/files/Land/UST/Documents/PDFfiles/Class_A_B_C_Training_Montana.pdf)


##### Free online TankHelper Training course for Class A and Class B Operators:
[TankHelper](https://www.montanamoodle.org/?tenant=tankhelper2)
  * [TankHelper instructions](https://deq.mt.gov/files/Land/UST/Documents/PDFfiles/TankhelperInstructions.pdf)
  * [Locate your corrective action training](https://deq.mt.gov/files/Land/UST/Documents/PDFfiles/LOCATINGCAPLessonsinTankHelper.pdf)


##### Class C Operator Training
Each designated Class C operator must either: be trained by a Class A or Class B operator; complete a training program; or pass a comparable examination. The training option chosen must teach or evaluate the Class C operator's knowledge to take appropriate actions (including notifying appropriate authorities) in response to emergencies or alarms caused by spills or releases resulting from the operation of the UST system. Class C operators must be trained before assuming duties. 
  * [Approved Class C Operator Training](https://deq.mt.gov/files/Land/UST/Documents/PDFfiles/ThirdPartyClass_C_operatortraining.pdf)
  * [Class C Operators Training Sheet](https://deq.mt.gov/files/Land/UST/Documents/PDFfiles/Class%20C%20operators%20form.pdf)
  * [Class C Operator Training and Orientation Checklist](https://deq.mt.gov/files/Land/UST/Documents/PDFfiles/MontanaUSTFacilityClassCOperatorTrainingChecklist.pdf)[](https://deq.mt.gov/files/Land/UST/Documents/PDFfiles/LOCATINGCAPLessonsinTankHelper.pdf)


[ List of Installers, Removers, and Testers ](https://deq.mt.gov/twr/assistance#accordion3-collapse5)
Owners or Operators of underground storage tank systems must obtain the services of a licensed UST system installer or remover for any installation, modification, or closure (removal) work.
  * [Installers/Removers](https://deq.mt.gov/files/Land/UST/Documents/MonthlyReportsPDF/LicensedInstallerRemovers.pdf)
  * [Remover Only](https://deq.mt.gov/files/Land/UST/Documents/MonthlyReportsPDF/LicensedRemovers.pdf?ver=2020-08-17-091541-923)


Some underground storage tank maintenance tests may be performed by individuals without Montana-specific licenses.
  * [](https://deq.mt.gov/files/Land/UST/Documents/PDFfiles/CATHODICPROTECTIONList.pdf)[Line and Tank Tightness Testers](https://deq.mt.gov/files/Land/UST/Documents/PDFfiles/LINETESTERSLIST.pdf): A periodic test of piping may be conducted only if it can detect a 0.1 gallon per hour leak rate at 1.5 times the operating pressure.
  * [Tank Tightness Testers](https://deq.mt.gov/files/Land/UST/Documents/PDFfiles/TANKTESTERSLIST.pdf): Tank tightness testing must be capable of detecting a 0.1 gallon per hour leak rate from any portion of the tank that routinely contains product while accounting for the effects of thermal expansion or contraction of the product, vapor pockets, tank deformation, evaporation or condensation, and the location of the water table.
  * [](https://deq.mt.gov/files/Land/UST/Documents/PDFfiles/TANKTESTERSLIST.pdf)[Ameron/Smith Certification List ](https://deq.mt.gov/files/Land/UST/Documents/MonthlyReportsPDF/AmeronSmithCertification.pdf)


[ List of Corrosion / Cathodic Protection Designers and Testers ](https://deq.mt.gov/twr/assistance#accordion3-collapse6)
**Note:** Montana law prohibits use of this information as a mailing list for unsolicited mass mailings, house calls or distributions or telephone calls.
**Disclaimer:** This list was prepared as an information source only. No endorsement of these firms or their services is given or implied. There may be other firms offering these services which at the date of this listing were not known by this agency.
##### Corrosion Protection
If your UST system, or any part of it, is protected by either sacrificial anodes or an impressed current system, the cathodic protection system must be tested at least every three years to make sure it is functioning properly. The required tests can be conducted by a qualified cathodic protection tester who will take soil potential readings, as directed by DEQ, and provide the tank owner with the results. The results should be kept at the facility.
##### Designers
  * [Cathodic Protection Designers](https://deq.mt.gov/files/Land/UST/Documents/PDFfiles/CATHODICPROTECTIONList.pdf)


##### Installers
  * [Cathodic Protection Installers](https://deq.mt.gov/files/Land/UST/Documents/MonthlyReportsPDF/LicensedCP.pdf)


##### Testers
  * [Cathodic Protection Testers](https://deq.mt.gov/files/Land/UST/Documents/MonthlyReportsPDF/CPTesters.pdf)


If you would like to have your name added, removed or modified on these lists, pleaseemail the UST Program(406) 444-5300.
[ Inactive Status ](https://deq.mt.gov/twr/assistance#accordion3-collapse7)
To Place an Active UST system into Inactive Status, an owner or operator can place an UST system into Inactive Status by notifying the UST Section on the appropriate[Notification of Inactive Status for Underground Storage Tanks Systems form](https://deq.mt.gov/files/Land/UST/Documents/PDFfiles/NotifyofInactiveStatus.pdf) that they have discontinued use of the system and wish the system to be considered inactive.
In addition, an owner/operator must:
  * Empty the tank to less than 1 inch of product (release detection is required until tank has less than 1 inch of product)
  * Cap and secure all product lines, sumps, manways, and ancillary equipment
  * Leave vent lines open and functioning
  * Continue to maintain corrosion protection (if applicable)
  * Continue to pay annual tank registration fees
  * Continue to demonstrate financial responsibility by having the[](https://deq.mt.gov/files/Land/UST/Documents/PDFfiles/CERTFR.pdf)
  * Continue to have a trained Class A, Class B, and Class C Operator
  * Conduct an inactive tank inspection every three years


Rules regarding Inactive Status, Permanent Closure, and Change in Service can be found in[Chapter 56, Subchapter 7, Administrative Rules of Montana](https://rules.mt.gov/gateway/Subchapterhome.asp?scn=17%2E56.7)
* * *
##### To Return an Inactive Tank System to Active Status
If an UST system has a valid Operating Permit and is in Inactive Status for12 Months or less:
  * An owner or operator must give the department 30 days advance**written**


If an UST system has a valid Operating Permit and is in Inactive Status for12 Months or more:
  * An owner or operator must give the department 30 days advance**written****, and**
  * Request a One Time Fill Permit
  * Perform a precision tank tightness test, line tightness tests and functionality tests of all mechanical and electronic release detection equipment, and submit all test results to the department. The owner or operator may return the UST system to active status only after the receipt of notice from the department indicating that the test results are satisfactory.


If an UST system does not have a valid Operating Permit andno more than 12 months have passed
  * An owner or operator must give the department 30 days advance**written****and**
  * Obtain a Conditional Operating Permit from the department (expires 180 days later),**and**
  * Have a compliance inspection conducted between 90 and 120 days after the Conditional Operating Permit is issued.


If an UST system does not have a valid Operating Permit andmore than 12 Months have passed
  * An owner or operator must give the department 30 days advance**written****and**
  * Request a One Time Fill Permit for testing purposes only,**and**
  * Perform a precision tank tightness test, line tightness tests and functionality tests of all mechanical and electronic release detection equipment, and submit all test results to the department. The owner or operator may return the UST system to active status only after the receipt of notice from the department indicating that the test results are satisfactory,**and**
  * Obtain a Conditional Operating Permit from the department (expires 180 days later),**and**
  * Have a compliance inspection conducted between 90 and 120 days after the Conditional Operating Permit is issued.


If an UST system does not have a valid Operating Permit andmore than 3 years have passed
  * An owner or operator must give the department 30 days advance**written****and**
  * Request a One Time Fill Permit for testing purposes only,**and**
  * Show that the UST is structurally sound based upon a program-approved integrity assessment,**and**
  * Perform a precision tank tightness test, line tightness tests and functionality tests of all mechanical and electronic release detection equipment, and submit all test results to the department. The owner or operator may return the UST system to active status only after the receipt of notice from the department indicating that the test results are satisfactory,**and**
  * Obtain a Conditional Operating Permit from the department (expires 180 days later),**and**
  * Have a compliance inspection conducted between 90 and 120 days after the Conditional Operating Permit is issued.


[ Closure and Removal ](https://deq.mt.gov/twr/assistance#accordion3-collapse8)
##### Requirements
  * Be sure that the tanks are registered with the Department.**The owner/operator must be current for payment of registration fees before the permit will be issued.**
  * Obtain the services of a licensed underground storage tank remover unless the owner/operator plans to close/remove the underground storage tank/piping.
  * A
  * Owners/operators who are closing/removing their own tanks must arrange for the local licensed inspector or a Department inspector to inspect their work. An inspection fee deposit of $90 must accompany the application and the inspection dates must be tentatively scheduled on the application. The fee deposit is for a two-hour inspection. The actual inspection fee will be the fee deposit plus $45 per-hour for all hours over two hours, including travel time and inspection time.
  * After DEQ reviews and approves the application, the owner/operator will be issued a permit. The permit authorizes the tank/piping removal as described in the application; however, the permit will have special conditions written on it that must be followed.**A permit must be issued before removal/closure work starts.**
  * Part of the tank or piping removal/closure process includes conducting a site assessment to determine whether a release has occurred. This requires collecting [soil or water samples](https://deq.mt.gov/cleanupandrec/Programs/petrocleanup#accordion1-collapse1#accordion1-collapse1) that must be sent to a laboratory. Specific requirements will be included on the permit.
  * In addition to meeting the EPA and Montana requirements for removals/closures, the procedures must also meet all requirements set forth in the Uniform Fire Code and be approved by the state and local fire officials. Tanks must be removed unless fire officials permit filling them in-place. If removal of the tank system is physically impossible or threatens adjoining structures, fire officials may permit filling the tanks and pipes in-place. **Fire official approval is necessary prior to DEQ issuing a permit**. A permit and site assessment are required regardless of closure method.
  * A permit expires six months from its date of issuance. If the work cannot be completed within that time frame, the department may issue a written extension upon request from the installer/remover.
  * If doing a closure in an area with a high groundwater table, check with DEQ's Water Protection Bureau if you need a construction dewatering permit: [Construction Dewatering General Permits](https://deq.mt.gov/water/assistance#cdgp)


##### Place a Tank System into Permanent Closure
Notify DEQ at least 30 days before beginning permanent closure.
  * An owner or operator must give the department advance**written**
  * Empty and clean the UST by removing all liquids and accumulated sludge. All tanks and connected piping must be removed from the ground or, if approved by the department, filled with an inert solid material. The department does not generally approve closure in place unless removal from the ground endangers adjacent structures.
  * Conduct a site assessment in accordance with[ARM 17.56.703.](https://rules.mt.gov/gateway/RuleNo.asp?RN=17%2E56%2E703)


[ Financial Responsibility ](https://deq.mt.gov/twr/assistance#accordion3-collapse9)
State regulations ([Title 17, Chapter 56, Subchapter 8, Administrative Rules of Montana](https://rules.mt.gov/gateway/Subchapterhome.asp?scn=17%2E56.8)) require Underground Storage Tanks (UST) owners and operators to demonstrate financial responsibility for the clean-up or third party liability compensation that results from a petroleum release from UST systems.
**Who must demonstrate financial responsibility?**
  * Owners or operators of petroleum UST systems must demonstrate financial responsibility for taking corrective action and for compensating third parties.


**What UST systems require demonstration of financial responsibility?**
  * Financial responsibility requirements apply to underground tanks and piping storing petroleum.


**There are two categories of exclusions that are listed below:**
  * State and federal owned UST systems,
  * Tanks excluded under ARM 17.56.102(2),(3),(4),(5) or (6).


* * *
##### Elements of Financial Responsibility (FR)
There are four primary elements that make up the financial responsibility requirements for Underground Storage Tank (UST) Systems. Owners or operators must demonstrate each of the following elements:
  * **Financial Responsibility mechanism** : This is the financial assurance option selected by owners/operators.
  * **Amount of financial responsibility** : This is the amount of coverage required that depends on the tank operator, number of tanks covered and name of issuer and mechanism number (if applicable).
  * **Scope of financial responsibility** : This is the financial responsibility mechanism that must cover the required categories of obligations and releases (e.g., corrective action and third-party compensation) and period of coverage.
  * **Certification** : This is the documentation that demonstrates the correct financial responsibility mechanisms, coverage amount and scope sufficient.


* * *
##### Financial Responsibility (FR) Types of Mechanisms
  * [**Financial Test of Self Insurance**](https://rules.mt.gov/gateway/RuleNo.asp?RN=17%2E56%2E807): A firm with a tangible net worth of at least $10 million may demonstrate FR by passing one of the two financial tests listed in the Montana Administrative Rules.
  * [**Guarantee**](https://rules.mt.gov/gateway/RuleNo.asp?RN=17%2E56%2E808): You may secure a corporate guarantee from another eligible firm. The provider of the guarantee must pass one of the financial tests listed in the Montana Administrative Rules.
  * [**Insurance Coverage and Risk Retention Group Coverage**](https://rules.mt.gov/gateway/RuleNo.asp?RN=17%2E56%2E809): You may buy insurance from an insurer or a risk retention group.
  * [**Surety Bond**](https://rules.mt.gov/gateway/RuleNo.asp?RN=17%2E56%2E810): You may obtain a surety bond that is a guarantee by a surety company that will satisfy FR obligations if the person who obtained the surety bond does not.
  * [**Letter of Credit**](https://rules.mt.gov/gateway/RuleNo.asp?RN=17%2E56%2E811): You may obtain a letter of credit that obligates the issuer to provide funding for corrective action and third-party compensation. If selecting this type, must also have a standby trust fund.
  * [**Montana Petroleum Tank Cleanup Fund**](https://rules.mt.gov/gateway/RuleNo.asp?RN=17%2E56%2E815): Petroleum Tank Release Cleanup Fund is a state-funded program.
  * [**Trust Fund**](https://rules.mt.gov/gateway/RuleNo.asp?RN=17%2E56%2E816): You may set up a fully funded trust fund administered by a third-party to pay for corrective action and third-party claims.
  * [**Standby Trust Fund**](https://rules.mt.gov/gateway/RuleNo.asp?RN=17%2E56%2E817): Standby trust that uses one of these assurance mechanisms (guarantee, surety bond or letter of credit) to fund the trust.


* * *
##### Amounts Required and/or Paid 
**Owner/Operator:**
1) Must have valid operating permit if using Petroleum Tank Release Compensation Board (PTRCB) funding  
a)**And****full regulatory compliance**  
b) If**not in full regulatory compliance**  
i) 1-30 days – 90 percent reimbursement  
ii) 31-60 days- 75 percent reimbursement  
iii) 61-90 days – 50 percent reimbursement  
iv) 91-180 days – 25 percent reimbursement  
v) Greater than 180 days = No reimbursement  
2) CO-PAY: If checking PTRCB must have other mechanism(s) checked to cover the co-pay requirement of $17,500 and costs in excess of $1 million  
a) If checking Letter of Credit, must also check and have Standby Trust Fund
  * for period explanation see[ARM 17.58.336 (7)](https://rules.mt.gov/gateway/RuleNo.asp?RN=17%2E58%2E336)


**PTRCF**
1) Must meet Owner/Operator requirements listed above  
2) PRTCF covers:  
a) **Per Occurrence** :  
i) 50 percent of the first $35,000 of eligible costs and 100 percent of subsequent eligible costs, up to a maximum total reimbursement of $982,500 for occurrences of a release.  
ii) 50 percent of the first $10,000 of eligible costs and 100 percent of subsequent eligible costs, up to a maximum total reimbursement of $495,000 for all tanks storing heating oil for consumptive use on the premises.  
b) **Annual Aggregate** :  
i) Federal Requirement: <= 100 tanks $1 million, OR > 100 tanks $2 million  
ii) The PTRCF covers each release or occurrence for $1 million less the required copay, without any aggregation (excluding tanks that are storing heating oil that is being consumed on the premises).
* * *
##### Scope of Financial Responsibility
**Obligations**
  * **Accidental** : Owner/Operator must be able to cover all UST system releases classified as accidental, as opposed to intentional releases.
  * **Sudden and Non-Sudden** : Owner/Operator must be able to cover both sudden and non-sudden UST system releases.


**Releases**
  * **Corrective Action** : FR must address both on and off site corrective action due to UST system releases.
  * **Third-Party Compensation** : FR mechanism must cover both bodily injury and property damage claims from third parties as a result of UST system releases.

[](https://deq.mt.gov/files/Land/UST/Documents/PDFfiles/Net%20Worth%20Blank.pdf)
* * *
##### Certificate of Financial Responsibility
  * [Montana Certificate of Financial Responsibility](https://deq.mt.gov/files/Land/UST/Documents/PDFfiles/CERTFR.pdf)


##### Financial Statement Forms
  * [Business Tangible Net Worth](https://deq.mt.gov/files/Land/UST/Documents/PDFfiles/Business%20Financial%20Statement.pdf)
  * [Tangible Net Worth for Sole Proprietorship](https://deq.mt.gov/files/Land/UST/Documents/PDFfiles/Net%20Worth.pdf)


* * *
##### Other Coverage
**Farm, Residence or Heating Oil Tanks**
Tanks on farms, residences or used for heating oil and that are <= 1,100 gallons
  * 100 percent eligible for costs up to $500,000 if properly designed and have double-walled system with release between 10/01/1993 to 10/01/2009, **or**
  * Owner needs $5,000 co-pay, then PTRCB pays 50 percent of first $10,000 and 100 percent of costs up to $495,000


##### Financial Responsibility Mechanisms for Local Governments
Local governments may use the financial responsibility mechanisms available to other owners and operators. However, they have the option of using four additional mechanisms that have been tailored to their characteristics.
  * Bond Rating Test: A local government may demonstrate (or guarantee) financial responsibility by passing a bond rating test.
  * Financial Test: A local government may demonstrate (or guarantee) by passing a financial test.
  * Guarantee: A local government may obtain from another local government or the State of Montana.
  * Dedicated Fund: A local government may demonstrate (or guarantee) financial responsibility by establishing a fund.


### More Info on Financial Responsibility
  * [Dollars and Sense (EPA)](https://www.epa.gov/ust/dollars-and-sense-financial-responsibility-requirements-underground-storage-tanks)
  * [Financial Responsibility for Underground Storage Tanks: A Reference Manual (EPA)](https://www.epa.gov/sites/production/files/2014-03/documents/frustman.pdf)
  * [List of Known Insurance Providers for Underground Storage Tank Owners and Operators (EPA)](https://deq.mt.gov/files/Land/UST/Documents/PDFfiles/listofknowninsuranceproviders.pdf)


[ Real Estate and Land Transactions ](https://deq.mt.gov/twr/assistance#accordion3-collapse10)
**Buyer disclaimer:**
  * When a person buys property, they are buying responsibility for any existing contamination as well.
  * Verify that all fees for your site are up to date with [EStop](https://svc.mt.gov/dor/estopportal/). 
  * [Environmental Site Assessments](https://www.tn.gov/content/dam/tn/environment/policy-planning/documents/opp_env-site-assessment-faqs.pdf). 


##### Non-regulated UST systems:
  * Farm or residential tanks (often, heating oil) for consumptive use on the property and
  * Less than 1,100 gallon capacity and
  * Installed before April 27, 1995


Even though these tanks are not regulated, any contamination they may have caused is regulated.
##### Regulated UST systems:
  * Any commercial underground storage tank
  * Any farm or residential underground storage tank installed after April 27, 1995
  * Any underground piping attached to aboveground tanks


##### If the tanks are gone:
The possibility of contamination is the chief concern. If the USTs were registered with the department and properly closed, the UST section may have record of the tank removal and copies of the sampling results. If not, a Phase II site assessment conducted by an environmental consultant offers the best protection.
A site assessment is only as good as the sampling. If the contamination is found on the property, it won't matter what previous results indicated. The contamination will need to be cleaned up.
##### If the tank(s) are still there and are not operational:
If the property houses tanks that were not properly closed in place, the department will require the owner to pull them and sample for contamination.
Compliance with UST regulations should be considered when buying land. Yet the first concern remains, "is the ground or groundwater contaminated?" Compliance with closure requirements will not alter that ground truth.
If a release is discovered, compliance
In many cases, the UST section cannot determine from information in the file whether they regulate a tank system or whether it is in compliance. If the tank was not registered with the department or if use was discontinued before November of 1988, we may have no information about the tank at all.
When the department learns of a tank's existence we will require that it be permanently and properly closed and that the ground beneath it be sampled for contamination. The owner of the property will be responsible for cleaning up the contamination under the direction of the [Petroleum Tank Cleanup Section](https://deq.mt.gov/cleanupandrec/Programs/petrocleanup).
A found or non-notified tank is considered "active" until the owner asks the department[Inactive Status](https://deq.mt.gov/twr/assistance#accordion3-collapse7) to learn more. PTRCB eligibility requires that an owner of a newly found tank submit a permit application to the UST section to pull the tank within 30 days of discovering it.
##### If the tank(s) are still there and operational:
The possibility of contamination should be considered. The owner of the property is responsible for cleaning up contamination regardless of who caused it and when. A Phase II site assessment conducted by an environmental consultant offers the best protection.
Compliance issues at operational facilities are addressed through third-party Compliance Inspections and Tank Status. Please visit [Mapping Resources and Tank Status](https://deq.mt.gov/twr/resources#accordion4-collapse6) for underground storage tank lists, permit status, and interactive maps. To review known leaking underground storage tanks (LUSTs), visit the [Petroleum Tank Cleanup page](https://deq.mt.gov/cleanupandrec/Programs/petrocleanup#accordion1-collapse4).
[ Aboveground Storage Tanks ](https://deq.mt.gov/twr/assistance#accordion3-collapse11)
**Aboveground Storage Tank (AST) Regulations**
  * Montana DEQ UST program regulates storage tank systems that fit the legal definition of an underground storage tank. That includes Aboveground Storage Tanks (ASTs) that have **connected underground product piping.** If an aboveground tank has underground piping, it is treated as an Underground Storage Tank (UST) and would be subject to Montana UST regulations. However, it does not include farm, residential tanks, or piping installed as of April 27, 1995 that have a capacity of 1,100 gallons or less that is used for storing motor fuel for noncommercial purposes or for storing heating oil for consumptive use on the premises where it is stored. [Review the definition of an UST per the MCA.](https://leg.mt.gov/bills/mca/title_0750/chapter_0110/part_0050/section_0030/0750-0110-0050-0030.html)
  * If the AST is completely above ground with above ground piping, then the AST is not regulated by the Montana DEQ UST program. ASTs are required to meet the State/Federal/International Fire Codes set forth by the [State Fire Marshal’s office](https://dojmt.gov/dci-home/investigations-bureau/fire-prevention-and-investigations/contact-information/). Cities and counties may also have regulations for ASTs so check with your local city and county for more information. Your local fire department, fire chief, or Deputy Fire Marshal is a good resource for the local regulations.
  * Facilities with aboveground storage tanks (ASTs) holding oils of any kind may be subject to U.S. EPA's [Spill Prevention, Control, and Countermeasure (SPCC) regulation](https://www.epa.gov/oil-spills-prevention-and-preparedness-regulations) (40 CFR Part 112). The SPCC regulation does not specifically use the term AST, but rather includes ASTs under the term bulk storage container. 
  * A bulk storage container is “any container used to store oil. These containers are used for purposes including, but not limited to, the storage of oil prior to use, while being used, or prior to further distribution in commerce. Oil-filled electrical, operating, or manufacturing equipment is not a bulk storage container.” Oil-filled equipment may also be subject to the SPCC regulation and should be included with the bulk storage container capacity when determining the facility’s overall aggregate oil storage capacity. For more information on whether SPCC applies to your facility or not, visit [EPA's website](https://www.epa.gov/oil-spills-prevention-and-preparedness-regulations/does-spill-prevention-control-and-countermeasure).
  * Aboveground storage tanks interested in applying for cleanup assistance from the Montana Petroleum Tank Release Cleanup Fund (PTRCF) are required to be in compliance with Administrative Rules of Montana (ARM) , [Chapter 17, subchapter 58](https://rules.mt.gov/gateway/chapterhome.asp?chapter=17%2E58), including specifics for ASTs outlined in [ARM 17.58.326](https://rules.mt.gov/gateway/RuleNo.asp?RN=17%2E58%2E326). For more information on applying for the fund, [visit the Petroleum Tank Release Compensation Board's webpage](https://deq.mt.gov/cleanupandrec/Programs/ptrcb#accordion1-collapse5).[](https://deq.mt.gov/cleanupandrec/Programs/ptrcb#accordion1-collapse5) Review the PTRCF checklists that serve as road maps to compliance and gaining eligibility to the Fund below.


  * [Storage Tank Checklist](https://deq.mt.gov/files/DEQAdmin/PET/Documents/Forms/StorageTankChecklist.pdf)
  * [Heating Oil Checklist](https://deq.mt.gov/files/DEQAdmin/PET/Documents/Forms/HeatingOilChecklist.pdf)


For more information on ASTs and PTRCF, call (406) 444-9710.

(1)(b) a list of persons prepared by the agency may not be used as a mailing list except by the agency or another agency without first securing the permission of those on the list.  
(9) a person violating the provisions of subsection (1)(b) is guilty of a misdemeanor.

[ Licensing Information and Continuing Education ](https://deq.mt.gov/twr/assistance#accordion4-collapse2)
The Montana DEQ UST Program licenses individuals as Compliance Inspectors, Installer/Removers, Junior Installers, Compliance Testers, Removers, and Corrosion Protection Installers.
**Compliance Inspectors:** may inspect UST systems for compliance with Montana DEQ UST regulations.
**Installer/Removers:** may install, replace, repair, modify, or close any component of a UST system.
**Junior Installers:** may install, replace, repair, or modify spill prevention equipment, containment sumps used for interstitial monitoring, and overfill prevention equipment and conduct all testing allowed under the Compliance Tester license.
**Compliance Testers:** may conduct testing of spill containment, overfill prevention equipment, and containment sumps used for interstitial monitoring.
**Removers:** may only close UST systems.
**CP Installers:** may only install and repair corrosion protection on UST systems.
Each license type requires specific prerequisite courses and professional references attesting to UST work experience. Successful applicants must pass the DEQ provided examination with a score of at least 80%. **Contact the UST Program with any questions or for more information regarding your licensing exam and license payment at 406-444-4656.**
In order to apply for a license, individuals must submit a license application, professional references, and examination and licensing fee of $483. All licenses will be issued for 3-year cycles. The current 3-year cycle is 3/1/2025 through 2/29/2028. At the end of the 3-year cycle individuals will be required to submit documentation of required continuing education courses and pay the 3-year licensing fee in order to renew.
**Application forms and references must be submitted via email to:****dequstprogram@mt.gov****. License fees may be paid through the online**[**eBill Payment Service**](https://svc.mt.gov/doa/opp/DEQeBill/cart)
**License Applications and Reference Forms**
Each license type requires a separate application and reference forms available below. Submit forms via email to the UST Program at dequstprogram@mt.gov.
  1. [UST Compliance Inspector Application](https://deq.mt.gov/files/Land/UST/Documents/PDFfiles/ComplianceInspctrAppForm.pdf)
  2. [UST Compliance Inspector Reference Form](https://deq.mt.gov/files/Land/UST/Documents/PDFfiles/CIReference.pdf)
  3. [UST Installer/Remover or Remover License Application](https://deq.mt.gov/files/Land/UST/Documents/PDFfiles/IRapp.pdf)
  4. [UST Junior Installer License Application](https://deq.mt.gov/files/Land/UST/Documents/PDFfiles/Junior%20Installer%20Application.pdf)
  5. [UST Compliance Tester License Application](https://deq.mt.gov/files/Land/UST/Documents/PDFfiles/Compliance%20Tester%20Application.pdf)
  6. [UST Corrosion Protection Installer Application](https://deq.mt.gov/files/Land/UST/Documents/PDFfiles/CPInstallerApp.pdf)
  7. [UST Consolidated Installer/Remover/CP/Tester Reference Form](https://deq.mt.gov/files/Land/UST/Documents/PDFfiles/IRreference.pdf)


* * *
**To become a licensed Compliance Inspector, an individual must:**
  1. Submit a completed UST Compliance Inspector Application electronically to DEQ.
  2. Submit three professional references. 
     * Each professional reference must complete and submit a [Compliance Inspector Application Reference Form](https://deq.mt.gov/files/Land/UST/Documents/PDFfiles/CIReference.pdf) _._
  3. Submit an examination and licensing fee of $483.
  4. Complete an inspector training curriculum approved by DEQ that includes training in the operation and maintenance of release detection, corrosion protection, spill and overfill equipment, and regulatory compliance. If the applicant possesses a Montana UST Installer License, this license meets the inspector training course requirement. Approved training courses include: 
     * [Purpora Engineering UST Boot Camp](https://purporaengineering.com/training-and-certification/)
     * [Alternative to UST Boot Camp Curriculum](https://deq.mt.gov/files/Land/UST/Documents/PDFfiles/AlternativeforBootCampfornewinspectors.pdf)
  5. Pass the State of Montana Compliance Inspector exam with a score of 80% or better.
  6. Pass a field practical exam, performing a compliance inspection at three UST facilities, administered by a State of Montana DEQ Compliance Inspector with a score of 80% or better.


[Compliance Inspector Study Guide Recommendations](https://deq.mt.gov/files/Land/UST/Documents/PDFfiles/ComplianceInspectorStudyGuideRecommendations.pdf)
* * *
**To become a licensed Installer/Remover, an individual must:**
  1. Submit a completed UST Installer/Remover application electronically to DEQ. 
     * The individual must have actively participated in at least three UST system installations and two UST system closures. 
  2. Submit an examination and licensing fee of $483.
  3. Submit three professional references attesting to the individual’s participation in UST system installations and closures.
  4. Each professional reference must complete and submit an [UST Consolidated Installer/Remover/CP/Tester Reference Form](https://deq.mt.gov/files/Land/UST/Documents/PDFfiles/IRreference.pdf) _.  
_
  5. Pass the State of Montana Installer/Remove exam with a score of 80% or better.


[Installer Study Guide Recommendations](https://deq.mt.gov/files/Land/UST/Documents/PDFfiles/InstallerStudyGuideRecommendations.pdf)
* * *
**To become a licensed Remover, an individual must:**
  1. Submit a completed UST Remover application electronically to DEQ. 
     * The individual must have actively participated in at least three UST system closures.
  2. Submit an examination and licensing fee of $483.
  3. Submit three professional references attesting to the individual’s participation in UST system closures.
  4. Each professional reference must complete and submit an [UST Consolidated Installer/Remover/CP/Tester Reference Form](https://deq.mt.gov/files/Land/UST/Documents/PDFfiles/IRreference.pdf) _._
  5. Pass the State of Montana UST System Remover exam with a score of 80% or better.


[Remover Study Guide Recommendations](https://deq.mt.gov/files/Land/UST/Documents/PDFfiles/RemoverStudyGuideRecommendations.pdf)
* * *
**To become a licensed Corrosion Protection Installer, an individual must:**
  1. Submit a completed UST Corrosion Protection Installer application electronically to DEQ. 
     * The individual must have actively participated in at least three UST system cathodic protection system installations or repairs.
  2. Submit an examination and licensing fee of $483.
  3. Submit three professional references attesting to the individual’s participation in UST corrosion protection projects.
  4. Each professional reference must complete and submit an [UST Consolidated Installer/Remover/CP/Tester Reference Form](https://deq.mt.gov/files/Land/UST/Documents/PDFfiles/IRreference.pdf) _._
  5. Pass the State of Montana Corrosion Protection Installer exam with a score of 80% or better.


[Corrosion Protection Installer Study Guide Recommendations](https://deq.mt.gov/files/Land/UST/Documents/PDFfiles/CathodicProtectionInstallerStudyRecommendations.pdf)
* * *
**To become a licensed Junior Installer, an individual must:**
  1. Submit a completed UST Junior Installer application electronically to DEQ. 
     * The individual must have at least 6 months of employment with a UST petroleum construction company and have actively participated in installations of spill containment, overfill prevention equipment, and containment sump repairs at five or more separate UST facilities.
  2. Submit an examination and licensing fee of $483.
  3. Submit two references attesting to the individual’s participation in required prerequisite UST installations, repairs, and/or replacements.
  4. Each professional reference must complete and submit an [UST Consolidated Installer/Remover/CP/Tester Reference Form](https://deq.mt.gov/files/Land/UST/Documents/PDFfiles/IRreference.pdf) _._
  5. Pass the State of Montana Junior Installer License exam with a score of 80% or better.


_Prerequisites for the Junior Installer licensing:_
  * The individual must have a minimum of six months of current employment with a UST petroleum construction company working under a Montana licensed Installer/Remover or Compliance Inspector.  
  

  * The individual must have two references attesting to the applicant’s UST work history including performing testing in accordance with PEI RP1200 including the use of the Montana UST Testing Forms. These two references must also attest to the applicant’s performing a minimum of: 
    1. Participated in work at a minimum of five (5) separate facilities
    2. Five (5) spill bucket installations, repairs, or replacements
    3. Three (3) overfill prevention device installations or replacements
    4. One (1) high level overfill alarm installation, repair, or replacement
    5. One (1) ball float vent valve removal
    6. Three (3) containment sump installations, repairs, or replacements


  * The individual must submit certifications for all the following: 
    1. UST Safety Course from an industry recognized organization 
      1. Example courses: Gilbarco-Veeder Root or Franklin Fueling Systems safety course
    2. Operation and programming of automatic tank gauges, compatible sensors, automatic line leak detectors (ELLD, PLLD, MLLD), and tank probes 
      1. Example Courses: Gilbarco-Veeder Root ATG Technician, Franklin Fueling Systems Compliance Testing Online Course
    3. Petroleum Equipment Institute RP1200 Testing of UST Spill, Overfill, Leak Detection and Secondary Equipment Online Test
    4. Manufacturer certifications for all equipment to be installed
    5. If the individual will be performing line tightness tests, they must submit a valid certification from the manufacturer of the specific testing equipment


[Junior Installer Study Guide Recommendations](https://deq.mt.gov/files/Land/UST/Documents/PDFfiles/Junior%20Installer%20Study%20Guide%20Recommendations.pdf)
  

* * *
**To become a licensed Compliance Tester, an individual must:**
  1. Submit a completed Compliance Tester application electronically to DEQ. 
     * The individual must have actively participated in at least three testing events on leak detection, spill containment, and overfill prevention components of UST systems.
  2. Submit an examination and licensing fee of $483.
  3. Submit two references attesting to the individual’s required prerequisite UST functionality testing on spill buckets, overfill prevention equipment and containment sumps. 
     * Each professional reference must complete and submit an [UST Consolidated Installer/Remover/CP/Tester Reference Form](https://deq.mt.gov/files/Land/UST/Documents/PDFfiles/IRreference.pdf) _._
  4. Pass the State of Montana Compliance Tester License exam with a score of 80% or better.


_Prerequisites for the Compliance Tester licensing:_
  * Must have a minimum of six months of current employment with a UST petroleum construction company working under a Montana licensed Installer/Remover or Compliance Inspector.  
  

  * Must have two references attesting to their UST work including performing testing in accordance with PEI RP1200 using Montana UST Testing Forms.  
  

  * The individual must submit certifications for all the following: 
    1. UST Safety Course from an industry recognized organization 
       * Example courses: Gilbarco-Veeder Root or Franklin Fueling Systems safety course
    2. Operation and programming of automatic tank gauges, compatible sensors, automatic line leak detectors (ELLD, PLLD, MLLD), and tank probes 
       * Example Courses: Gilbarco-Veeder Root ATG Technician, Franklin Fueling Systems Compliance Testing courses
    3. Petroleum Equipment Institute RP1200 Testing of UST Spill, Overfill, Leak Detection and Secondary Equipment Online Test
    4. Manufacturer certifications for all equipment to be tested
    5. If the applicant will be performing line tightness tests, they must submit a valid certification from the manufacturer of the testing equipment


[Compliance Inspector Study Guide Recommendations](https://deq.mt.gov/files/Land/UST/Documents/PDFfiles/ComplianceInspectorStudyGuideRecommendations.pdf)
* * *
**Continuing Education Courses**
At the end of each triennial period, renewal of all licenses will require documentation that a licensee has completed the required continuing education hours. Compliance Inspectors, Compliance Testers, Junior Installers, Corrosion Protection Installers, and Installer/Removers must complete at least two DEQ approved or sponsored continuing education courses for a total of 16 credit hours (8 credit hours must be a DEQ sponsored refresher training course). Licensed UST Removers must complete one DEQ sponsored refresher training course for a total of 4 credit hours.
**Continuing Education Requirements** **  
License Type** |  **Minimum Approved Courses Required** |  **Minimum Credit Hours Required**  
---|---|---  
Installer/Remover |  2 |  16 hours  
Remover |  1 |  4 hours  
Compliance Inspector |  2 |  16 hours  
Corrosion Protection Installer |  2 |  16 hours  
Compliance Tester |  2 |  16 hours  
Junior Installer |  2 |  16 hours

[Online Training : FFS PRO University](https://university.ffspro.com/student/page/1253866-online-training)
If you have attended a training that is not on this list, contact the department to seek course approval.
_The DEQ will make reasonable accommodations for persons with disabilities who wish to participate in this class or who need an alternative accessible format of this notice. If you require an accommodation, please contact our office at 406-444-5300._
[ When do I need a permit? ](https://deq.mt.gov/twr/assistance#accordion4-collapse3)
**The following UST activities in Montana require a permit from DEQ**(Note: This list is not all inclusive and if the action is not listed, contact the UST Program for guidance.)
  * Removing, closing in place, or changing in service of underground storage tanks or piping (product or vent)
  * Working on an UST system that requires excavation or cutting the concrete. This includes work on tanks, piping (product or vent), corrosion protection, automatic tank gauge (ATG), interstitial sensors, monitoring wells, lining, proving structural integrity, risers, pipes, spill and overfill.
  * Installing or changing an overfill device (i.e. drop tube shut off (flapper valve), overfill alarm). Includes removing ball float vent valve.
  * Replacing or repairing a damaged spill bucket or spill containment that failed testing. Includes replacement of the primary and/or secondary of a spill bucket due to a failed spill bucket test. Replacement or removal of a spill bucket drain plug does not require a permit.
  * Installing a **straight** drop tube. Note: replacing a straight drop tube requires a permit only if cutting the concrete or excavation is required.
  * Reconstructing, abandoning, or closing leak detection monitoring wells. These wells are no longer an approved leak detection method after October 13, 2023.
  * Installing an automatic tank gauge (ATG, an in-tank leak detection device) or interstitial sensors, whether or not the conduit and riser were previously installed.
  * Installing heat-shrink sleeves or water-tight boots to risers or flex connectors for corrosion protection.
  * Installing off-set sleeves to risers for corrosion protection. Offset sleeves must be non-corrodible (offset sleeves do not provide corrosion protection for flex connectors).
  * Installing sacrificial anodes on risers or flex connectors for corrosion protection.
  * Installing additional anodes or an impressed-current system on an existing UST system. This includes repairing or replacing components including rectifiers, wiring, anodes, etc.
  * Installing dri-sump tubing and test equipment.
  * Conducting an internal inspection (entering a tank) of a tank with the exception of using cameras.
  * Replacing a UST system component with a different model component.
  * Extending an existing vent standpipe.
  * Containment sump repair or replacement including crack repair due to visual failure. This includes repair or replacement of product or electrical penetration fittings.
  * Adding a solenoid or anti-siphon device.
  * Installation of an auto-dialer.


**The following UST activities do not require a permit but may require a Montana license to perform:**
  * Replacing a defective mechanical line leak detector with the same make and model device.
  * Replacing a defective electronic line leak detector with the same make and model device.
  * Testing tank tightness or line tightness testing.
  * Testing line leak detector functionality.
  * Testing piping to determine if it is U.S. Suction or safe suction.
  * Replacing an **existing** straight drop tube (without flapper valve).
  * Testing containment sump or interstitial sensor functionality.
  * Testing of ATG functionality by factory trained technician.
  * Replacing a drop tube overfill device with the same make and model. Replacing an existing overfill alarm with the same make and model.
  * Testing of a CP system by a qualified CP tester.
  * Adjusting of a CP system rectifier by a CP tester.
  * Replacing a probe or sensor with the same model.
  * Replacement or removal of a spill bucket drain plug.
  * Replacement or relocation of an Emergency Stop button


[ Construction Permits (Installation, Modification, Repairs) ](https://deq.mt.gov/twr/assistance#accordion4-collapse4)
**You must obtain a permit prior to conducting any UST system work, including installation, modification, and repair.**
All permits are subject to and must follow Montana specific[Critical Installation Requirements](https://deq.mt.gov/files/Land/UST/Documents/PDFfiles/NewApplications/2021_Critical_Installation_Elements_Revised.pdf). In addition, detailed information can be obtained in the[How to Submit a Completed Permit Application](https://deq.mt.gov/files/Land/UST/Documents/PDFfiles/NewApplications/How%20to%20Submit%20a%20Complete%20Application.pdf)
  * [Permit Checklist](https://deq.mt.gov/files/Land/UST/Documents/PDFfiles/PermitChecklist.pdf)


##### New Facility Installation (Montana DEQ recommends utilizing an environmental consultant to complete new facility applications):
  * [New Facility Permit Application](https://deq.mt.gov/files/Land/UST/Documents/PDFfiles/NewFacilityPermitapplication.pdf)
  * [Programmatic Environmental Assessment ](https://deq.mt.gov/files/Land/UST/Documents/PDFfiles/NewApplications/EnvironmentalAssessment.pdf)
  * [Sump Test Water Characterization and Disposal](https://www.epa.gov/ust/underground-storage-tank-technical-compendium-about-2015-ust-regulation#spillbuckets:~:text=UST%20Sump%20Test%20Water%20Characterization%20and%20Disposal)


##### Install:
  * [Major Installation](https://deq.mt.gov/files/Land/UST/Documents/PDFfiles/NewApplications/PermitApplicationMajor.pdf)
  * [Minor Installation](https://deq.mt.gov/files/Land/UST/Documents/PDFfiles/NewApplications/Permit%20Application%20Minor.pdf)
  * [UST System Certificate of Compliance](https://deq.mt.gov/files/Land/UST/Documents/PDFfiles/CertOfComp2014.pdf)


##### Supplements:
  * [Supplement A -**Tank Installation**](https://deq.mt.gov/files/Land/UST/Documents/PDFfiles/NewApplications/Supplement%20A%20Tank.pdf)
  * [Supplement B -**Pipe Installation**](https://deq.mt.gov/files/Land/UST/Documents/PDFfiles/NewApplications/Supplement%20B%20Pipe.pdf)
  * [Supplement C -**Tank And Pipe Installation**](https://deq.mt.gov/files/Land/UST/Documents/PDFfiles/NewApplications/Supplement%20C%20Tank%20and%20Piping.pdf)
  * [Supplement D -**Modification Repair, Lining, or Investigating**](https://deq.mt.gov/files/Land/UST/Documents/PDFfiles/NewApplications/Supplement%20D%20Modification.pdf)
  * [Supplement E -**Corrosion Protection Installation**](https://deq.mt.gov/files/Land/UST/Documents/PDFfiles/NewApplications/Supplement%20E%20CP.pdf)


**Closure:**
  * [Closure Permit](https://deq.mt.gov/files/Land/UST/Documents/PDFfiles/NewApplications/Permit%20Application%20Closure.pdf)
  * [UST System Closure Site Plan](https://deq.mt.gov/files/Land/UST/Documents/PDFfiles/ClosureSitePlan.pdf)
  * [UST System Tank Piping Closure Form](https://deq.mt.gov/files/Land/UST/Documents/PDFfiles/TankPipingClosureForm.pdf)
  * [UST System Closure Check List Requirements](https://deq.mt.gov/files/Land/UST/Documents/PDFfiles/ClosureChecklistRequirements.pdf)
  * [Summary of Underground Storage Tank Systems Closure Requirements](https://deq.mt.gov/files/Land/UST/Documents/PDFfiles/SummaryofClosureRequirements.pdf)
  * [Certification of Compliance](https://deq.mt.gov/files/Land/UST/Documents/PDFfiles/CertOfComp.pdf)
  * [List of Approved Analytical Labs](https://deq.mt.gov/files/Land/LUST/Documents/downloadables/Lab%20Contacts.pdf)
  * [Guidance for Disposal of Wastes Associated With Underground Storage Tank Removal](https://deq.mt.gov/files/Land/UST/Documents/PDFfiles/USTWasteGuidance.pdf)
  * [RBCA Guidance-Soil sampling and Water sampling ](https://deq.mt.gov/cleanupandrec/Programs/petrocleanup#accordion1-collapse1)


These procedures must be followed to obtain approval to install or modify underground storage tanks and piping in Montana:
  * Obtain the services of a licensed installer.
  * A
  * Please make all checks and money orders payable to the Department of Environmental Quality - UST Section.
  * **After the Department approves the application** , the owner/operator will be issued a permit. The permit authorizes the installation of the tank/piping as described in the application; however, the permit may have special conditions written on it that must also be followed.**A permit must be issued by the Department before installation begins.**
  * A permit expires six months from its date of issuance. If the work cannot be completed within that time frame, the department may issue a written extension upon request from the installer/remover.


##### Other considerations for installation:
  * The tank and piping system must meet all the design and leak detection requirements of the Montana Underground Storage Tank Rules and Regulations. A permit application for the modification or installation of a cathodic protection system must be accompanied by designs prepared by acorrosion expert.
  * The tank and piping system must be tested and installed in accordance with Division VI of the Uniform Fire Code.
  * Installation procedures must also comply with all local permit requirements and local ordinances. Check with local health and fire departments prior to completing the permit application.
  * If the proposed installation is in an environmentally sensitive area (in or close to groundwater or porous soils, near private or public water wells, etc.), we recommend that the best available leak prevention systems be installed. This will help minimize the potential liability and cost of a leak in the future. These systems could include the use of double walled tanks, vaults or excavation pit liners with monitoring well and leak detectors.
  * If doing installation in an area with a high groundwater table, check with DEQ's Water Protection Bureau if you need a construction dewatering permit: [Construction Dewatering General Permits](https://deq.mt.gov/water/assistance#cdgp)


If you need more information concerning the installation or modification of underground storage tanks and piping or have questions regarding the permit application procedure, contact the UST program.
[ Sump Directions and Testing ](https://deq.mt.gov/twr/assistance#accordion4-collapse5)
##### **EPA Sump Testing Information**
  * [Sump Test Water Characterization and Disposal](https://www.epa.gov/ust/underground-storage-tank-technical-compendium-about-2015-ust-regulation#spillbuckets:~:text=UST%20Sump%20Test%20Water%20Characterization%20and%20Disposal)


##### **Dri Sump**
  * [Dri-Sump Directions and Form](https://deq.mt.gov/files/Land/UST/Documents/MT%20Dri-sump%20directions%20and%20form%20FEB%202021.pdf)


##### **Low Level Sump**
**All Low Level containment sump testing requires DEQ UST program approval before testing.**
The licensed individual must submit an electronic request to DEQ at: DEQUSTProgram@mt.gov and wait for a program response.[Certification For Performing Low Level Containment Sump Testing](https://deq.mt.gov/files/Land/UST/Documents/LowLevelSumpTest/CERTIFICATION_2022.pdf) to see if you are able to proceed.
All sump testing is required to be completed by a state licensed individual.
  * [Low Level Sump Testing Procedure](https://deq.mt.gov/files/Land/UST/Documents/LowLevelSumpTest/LLS_Procedure_2022.pdf)
  * [Low Level Sump Testing Form](https://deq.mt.gov/files/Land/UST/Documents/LowLevelSumpTest/Low%20Level%20Sump%20Testform%20C-4A.pdf)


**Important:** The sumps must meet the conditions specified in the “Low Liquid Level UST Containment Sump Testing Procedures” guidance document to use this test method and comply with the requirements of 40 CFR 280.35(1)(ii)(C). If the sumps do not meet these requirements, you may not conduct low level sump testing. **You must answer yes to all of the questions below before proceeding:**
  1. Are all liquid sensors mounted and remain located at the lowest point in each containment sump?
  2. Has the functionality of each liquid level sensor been tested in conjunction with the low-level sump test and verified that each sensor works correctly and shuts down the appropriate pump or dispenser?
  3. Does the pump automatically shut off when liquid activates the sensor? Or does the dispenser automatically shut off when liquid activates the sensor, AND the facility is always staffed when the pumps are operational?
  4. Are the sensors programmed to both alarm and shut down when in contact with any liquid?


[ Facility Testing Dates ](https://deq.mt.gov/twr/assistance#accordion4-collapse6)
Test date data may not be accurate. This report only shows data that has been received by DEQ and entered into the database.
  * [Facility testing dates ](https://deq.mt.gov/files/Land/UST/Documents/MonthlyReportsPDF/rptUST_TankTestDates.pdf)

# Air Quality Monitoring 
  1. [Home](https://deq.mt.gov/)
  2. [Air](https://deq.mt.gov/air/index)
  3. Programs
  4. Air Quality Monitoring


### Program Overview
The purpose of Montana’s ambient air monitoring network is to monitor, assess and provide information on statewide air quality conditions and trends as specified by the Montana and Federal Clean Air Acts. The Air Quality Monitoring Program works in conjunction with local air pollution agencies and some industries, measuring air quality throughout Montana. This data provides the factual basis for regulatory decisions as well as provides air quality information to our local counterparts and the public.
##### [ Today's Air  ](http://todaysair.mtdeq.us)
##### [ Monitoring Data  ](https://discover-mtdeq.hub.arcgis.com/datasets/7ff8441bb7014aa1b8a1da58f51c16db_0/explore?location=46.548964%2C-109.995100%2C7.00&showTable=true)
[ Today's Air Information ](https://deq.mt.gov/air/Programs/monitoring#accordion1-collapse1)
Today's Air displays particulate concentrations and health effects for particulate matter less than 2.5 microns in diameter. This information is measured by monitoring equipment located throughout the state. The NowCast air quality concentrations are measured against health effect categories to provide a snapshot of the severity of impacts in your location. Concentrations are updated at 15 minutes past the hour at all stations. The most recent data displayed is the average particulate concentration measured over the previous hour.
For those counties with air quality programs using averaging times beyond the 1-hour average concentration of PM2.5in issuing air quality alerts, an alternate data tool is under development which will address these data needs and is scheduled for release around the mid-December timeframe. Additional information regarding this tool will be provided in the near future. In the meantime, 1-hour average PM2.5 ambient monitoring data from all stations is available from Today’s Air or the
* * *
#### Recommendations for Outdoor Activities
Wildfire season can frequently coincide with outdoor activities. The Departments of Health and Human Services and Environmental Quality have developed[](http://deq.mt.gov/Portals/112/Air/FireUpdates/Documents/Activity%20Guidelines%20for%20Wildfire%20Smoke%20Events.pdf)guide to help you make decisions about outdoor sporting events when air quality is a concern. Visit the [Air Quality and Smoke](https://deq.mt.gov/air/Programs/airquality) page for more information.
[ PurpleAirs in Schools ](https://deq.mt.gov/air/Programs/monitoring#accordion1-collapse2)
Free air quality sensors for your school to monitor Montana’s air pollutant of greatest concern, particulate matter (PM2.5).
Montana Department of Environmental Quality and the University of Montana Center for Population Health Research (UM CPHR) have partnered to arm high schools with free PM2.5 air quality sensors, one indoor and one outdoor so schools can improve decision making regarding the health and safety of students, student athletes, and staff. The data resulting from the indoor sensor will be kept private while the outdoor sensor data will be public and eventually displayed on [Today’s Air](https://gis.mtdeq.us/portal/apps/experiencebuilder/experience/?id=000f42b119c44c7f9c3b4336470c721e).
Additionally, this initiative focuses on filling the air quality data gaps in rural and underserved Montana communities to improve access to local air quality data and facilitate better public health messaging. There are no reporting requirements or enforcement attached to this opportunity. The PurpleAirs in Schools Project wants to provide communities with the technology to help minimize Montanan’s exposure to harmful particulate matter pollution.
Contact Keri Nauman at Montana DEQ with any questions.
[PurpleAirs in Schools Flyer](https://deq.mt.gov/files/Air/AirMonitoring/Documents/PurpleAirs%20in%20Schools%20Flyer.pdf)
[ Network Review Plan ](https://deq.mt.gov/air/Programs/monitoring#accordion1-collapse3)

# Colstrip Ash Pond Cleanup
  1. [Home](https://deq.mt.gov/)
  2. [Cleanup & Reclamation](https://deq.mt.gov/cleanupandrec/index)
  3. Programs
  4. Colstrip Coal Ash Pond Cleanup


## Program Overview
The Department of Environmental Quality is engaged in an enforcement action against Talen Energy (formerly PPL Montana) over groundwater contamination caused by leaking ash ponds at the Colstrip Steam Electric Stations. The steps Talen must take to address these problems are outlined in the [Administrative Order on Consent](https://deq.mt.gov/Files/DEQAdmin/MFS/Colstrip/COLSTRIPAOCFINALOFFICIALRECORD.pdf), which both parties agreed to in 2012.
This webpage includes the documents, maps, and other analyses of the Colstrip site related to the AOC. Because this enforcement action involves complicated legal and environmental factors, DEQ has prepared a set of frequently asked questions about the Colstrip facility (FAQs). We recommend that anyone interested in DEQ's work at the Colstrip facility refer to the FAQs first.
To stay up to date with DEQ's work at the Colstrip facility, or with other questions, email us at: deqcolstrip@mt.gov. We will add you to an email list which receives updates on public notices and other major announcements about this project.
#### Related Links
[Talen's CCR Website](https://www.talenenergy.com/ccr-colstrip/)
[More Information on the Montana Major Facility Siting Act](https://deq.mt.gov/energy/assistance)
#### Colstrip Coal Ash Pond Cleanup Contacts

deqcolstrip@mt.gov
